summary: Gruber examines why iMessage serves as a powerful retention mechanism keeping users on iPhone, even those who otherwise live in Google's ecosystem. He acknowledges that many tech-savvy iPhone users rely primarily on Google services, making them potential targets for Google's Pixel phones, but argues iMessage bridges both Apple-ecosystem and Google-ecosystem iPhone users. While others dismiss the idea of iMessage for Android as obviously contrary to Apple's interests, Gruber reveals that detailed mockups have circulated within Apple and argues the number of users who'd switch away would be negligible. He contends Apple should win by creating devices people want to use, not ones they're locked into, and that iMessage exclusivity is more crutch than strategic advantage.
key_points: Many tech-industry iPhone users live primarily in Google's ecosystem, making them the true target audience for Pixel phones
iMessage uniquely bridges Apple-ecosystem and Google-ecosystem iPhone users, giving it network effects that Google's Allo and Duo cannot replicate
iMessage gained traction by seamlessly replacing SMS, while FaceTime introduced genuinely new mass-market functionality — two different paths to stickiness
Detailed iMessage-for-Android mockups have circulated within Apple, suggesting the idea is not dismissed internally
Apple should win through desire, not obligation — platform-exclusive lock-in is a crutch, not a strategy
money_quotes: Apple wins by creating devices and experiences that people want to use, not that they have to use. Apple creates desire, not obligation.
If the iPhone isn't thriving simply by being the best, then Apple is already in deep trouble.
I would argue that in some ways Apple might be better off releasing iMessage for Android, simply to remove a crutch.
The way Apple usurped SMS for their own users and let SMS remain as a fallback for texting with everyone else was simply genius.
There's nothing inadvertent about iMessage's success.
iMessage for Android would surely lead some number of iPhone users to switch to Android, but I think that number is small enough to be a rounding error for Apple.
Another way to put it: if the Pixels don't get Google employees who use iPhones to switch, nothing will.
Apple doesn't need to rely on platform-exclusive lock-in.
key_insight: Apple's strength should come from making products people desire, not from platform lock-in — iMessage exclusivity is a crutch that masks whether the iPhone is winning on its own merits.
